text.title: CI Launch Flags, Timeouts, and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Required Linux CI launch flags, timeout discipline, memory/cleanup rules, managed browser service routing, and agent-specific failure patterns.

rule.statement: In Linux CI containers, always launch Chrome with --disable-dev-shm-usage --disable-gpu --no-sandbox; never use these flags on a developer workstation without a trusted container boundary.
rule.rationale: --no-sandbox disables the browser sandbox which is a security boundary; safe inside a dedicated CI container but dangerous on a shared machine. --disable-dev-shm-usage prevents /dev/shm exhaustion in Docker's 64MB default.

rule.statement: Set page.setDefaultTimeout(15000) at launch; never use waitForTimeout(N) as a substitute for waitForSelector or waitForLoadState.
rule.rationale: waitForTimeout is a fixed sleep; it fails on slow networks and wastes time on fast ones. waitForSelector blocks until the element appears, adapting to actual page state.

antipattern.description: Agent adds await page.waitForTimeout(2000) after every interaction to "let the page settle".
antipattern.reason: Fixed sleeps are fragile and slow; they fail when pages take longer than 2s and waste time when pages settle in 50ms. Use event-driven waits instead.

rule.statement: Always close the browser in a try/finally block; capture trace and screenshot on failure and discard on success to prevent disk accumulation.
rule.rationale: Agents that forget browser.close() in error paths leave zombie Chrome processes that exhaust memory. Keeping all traces accumulates gigabytes per CI run.

rule.statement: Route to a managed browser service (Browserless, Browserbase, Bright Data) when running more than 20 parallel sessions; local Chrome instances at that scale cause OOM and CPU contention.
rule.rationale: Each Chromium instance uses 100-300MB RAM; 20 parallel instances = 2-6GB RAM, exceeding standard CI runner limits and causing cascading failures.
